definition: A digital unit that can represent value, access, or voting power in a blockchain project.
A computer that helps a blockchain network store, check, or share transaction data.
A digital asset native to its own blockchain, often used to pay network fees.
To create new tokens or record a new digital asset on a blockchain.
A shared supply of tokens that traders or borrowers can use through a protocol.
To make tokens unavailable for a period, often to secure a network or earn rewards.
A batch of verified transactions added as one new record in a blockchain.
A change to blockchain rules that can create a new version of the network.
A fixed-length digital fingerprint used to identify and protect blockchain data.
The fee paid to process a transaction or run an action on a blockchain.
The linked record of transaction blocks that forms a blockchain's history.
The return earned from putting digital assets to work in a DeFi protocol.
A shared record of balances and transactions that a blockchain keeps in sync.
Another participant in a network that communicates directly without a central server.
To lock tokens to help secure a network or protocol, usually in return for rewards.
To exchange one token for another, often through a decentralized exchange.
To permanently remove tokens from circulation by sending them where they cannot be spent.
A smart-contract strategy that manages deposited assets to pursue a return.
Software or hardware that manages the keys used to control blockchain assets.
A participant that uses computing work to validate transactions on some blockchains.
